one_line_takeaway: A four-task empirical study showing open-source LLMs, once fine-tuned, can match or beat small pre-trained LMs at vulnerability assessment and line-location but lag dedicated approaches at detection and (nominally) at description, where their weakness is mostly an over-generation artifact rather than lack of understanding.
key_finding: LLMs are NOT uniformly good vulnerability analysts. For detection, fine-tuned LLMs (F1 0.214-0.270) trail transformer-based approaches; SVulD leads at F1 0.336 / Precision 0.282 / Accuracy 0.915. For assessment and location, however, fine-tuned code LLMs beat pre-trained LMs: DeepSeek-Coder 6.7B tops assessment (F1 0.814, Acc 0.860) and WizardCoder/CodeLlama top location (F1 0.520/0.504 vs best LM CodeT5 0.493). For description, LLMs are markedly weaker (fine-tuned avg ROUGE-1 0.406 vs LMs' 0.600), but a trimming experiment shows this is largely an over-generation artifact: CodeLlama's ROUGE-1 jumps from 0.366 to 0.625 once excess output is cut, nearing CodeT5+ (0.730). Fine-tuning is essential across all tasks; few-shot performance is poor everywhere.
core_contribution: An empirical evaluation pipeline (by Yin, Ni, and Wang) that measures the multi-task ability of six open-source LLMs on FOUR software-vulnerability activities using a single dataset (Big-Vul): (1) vulnerability detection, (2) vulnerability assessment/severity rating, (3) vulnerability location (line-level), and (4) vulnerability description generation. The paper's own contribution is the systematic head-to-head comparison of open-source code and general LLMs against (a) pre-trained small language models and (b) prior SOTA vulnerability approaches, under BOTH few-shot and fine-tuning settings. It explicitly distinguishes itself from cited prior work (Zhou et al. [33], Fu et al. [34]) which only studied closed-source ChatGPT via prompting; this paper studies open-source LLMs under reproducible few-shot AND fine-tuning settings and releases a replication package.
threat_model: Not an attacker-vs-defender threat model. The security framing is defensive tooling: LLMs are evaluated as automated aids for software quality assurance across the vulnerability lifecycle (detecting vulnerable C/C++ functions, rating CVSS v2.0 severity, localizing vulnerable lines, and writing CVE-style descriptions). Ground truth is real-world CVE-linked vulnerabilities from open-source projects. There is no adversarial/evasion component; the question is whether LLMs are competent vulnerability analysts, not whether they resist attack.
limitations: Only Big-Vul (C/C++, CVEs 2002-2019) is used, so generalization to recent vulnerabilities, other languages, and manually-built datasets like SARD is untested (authors acknowledge this as an external-validity threat). Fine-tuning limited to <=7B and few-shot to <=34B due to compute (10x RTX 3090). Detection results are on a heavily imbalanced test set (5.5-6.1% vulnerable), so F1 numbers are low across ALL methods. A notable methodological caveat surfaced by the authors themselves: their reproduced LineVul scores F1 0.272 vs the original paper's 0.910 -- traced to their space-trimming preprocessing step, which drastically harms transformer-based token-level models (LineVul drops to 0.40, UniXcoder to 0.26 after space removal). This means the detection leaderboard is sensitive to a preprocessing choice and cross-paper comparisons should be read cautiously. Assessment uses CVSS v2.0 (dated). Description metric is ROUGE (n-gram overlap), which penalizes verbose-but-correct LLM output.

datasets_used: Big-Vul (Fan et al.) -- 3,754 vulnerabilities, 348 open-source projects, 91 CWE types, 2002-2019; 188,636 C/C++ functions with 10,900 (5.7%) vulnerable
Split: Training 17,440 (balanced 8,720/8,720 via undersampling); Validation 18,864 (1,090 vul); Testing 18,864 (1,090 vul, natural ~6.1% ratio)
quantitative_results: Detection (RQ1): best baseline SVulD F1 0.336, Precision 0.282, Accuracy 0.915; fine-tuned LLM best DeepSeek-Coder 6.7B F1 0.270; fine-tuned LLM F1 range 0.214-0.270; few-shot LLM F1 only 0.084-0.128; graph-based baselines F1 0.200-0.232
Detection reproduction caveat: LineVul reproduced F1 0.272 vs original 0.910; space-removal preprocessing drops LineVul to F1 0.40 and UniXcoder to F1 0.26

Assessment (RQ2): DeepSeek-Coder 6.7B fine-tuned best F1 0.814 / Recall 0.785 / Precision 0.854 / Accuracy 0.860; best LM CodeBERT F1 0.753; adding key context info raises accuracy range from 0.26-0.42 to 0.27-0.56
Location (RQ3): WizardCoder 7B F1 0.520 and CodeLlama 7B F1 0.504 (Precision 0.691, FPR 0.021) beat best LM CodeT5 F1 0.493; CodeLlama fine-tuning lifts F1 from 0.082 (few-shot) to 0.504

Description (RQ4): pre-trained LMs avg ROUGE-1/2/L 0.600/0.487/0.591 vs fine-tuned LLMs 0.406/0.301/0.400; CodeT5+ best at 0.747/0.668/0.740; CodeLlama-Trim experiment on 100 samples raised ROUGE-1 from 0.366 to 0.625 (CodeT5+ 0.730)
Input-length effect: StarCoder/Phi-2 assessment F1 decline as input grows (StarCoder 0.29 at 0-128 tokens down to 0.25 at 1024+)
baselines_compared: Pre-trained LMs: CodeBERT (125M), GraphCodeBERT (125M), UniXcoder (125M), PLBART (140M), CodeT5 (220M), CodeT5+ (220M)
Vulnerability-specific approaches: Devign, Reveal, IVDetect (graph-based); LineVul, SVulD (transformer-based)
LLMs under comparison also serve as each other's baselines across few-shot vs fine-tuning settings
